steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence reads: "they exhibit striking differences in ______ object 39P/Oterma is considered an active centaur..., 83982 Crantor...is considered dormant." The blank must close the noun phrase "differences in [noun]" and then introduce two independent clauses that contrast the two centaur objects. The punctuation must both end the noun phrase cleanly and separate the two independent clauses that follow.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'behavior: while the': "behavior" completes the noun phrase. The colon correctly introduces the elaboration. "While" is a subordinating conjunction that sets up a contrast between the two objects, creating one complex sentence rather than a comma splice. ✓
  • Choice B — 'behavior; the': The semicolon closes the noun phrase correctly, but what follows is "the object 39P/Oterma is...active centaur, 83982 Crantor...is dormant" — two independent clauses joined only by a comma, which is a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — 'behavior, while the': A comma is too weak to close the noun phrase and mark the boundary before the elaborating clauses. The sentence needs a colon or semicolon at this structural break. ✗
  • Choice D — 'behavior. The': A period here would make "they exhibit striking differences in behavior" a complete sentence, but then "The object 39P/Oterma is...active centaur, 83982 Crantor...is dormant" contains two independent clauses joined by only a comma — again a comma splice. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
